title: Half-life and uses of radiation
examTip: Half-life calculations: each half-life halves the activity. After n half-lives, remaining activity = initial × (1/2)ⁿ. Read this off a graph or calculate — the exam often gives you a decay graph and asks you to determine the half-life by finding how long it takes for activity to drop from one value to exactly half.
introTips: Radioactive decay is random — you cannot predict when any individual nucleus will decay, but with large numbers the overall rate is very predictable.
Half-life is the time for half the undecayed nuclei to decay — equivalently, the time for activity to halve.
A short half-life means the substance decays quickly — useful for medical tracers; a long half-life means it stays radioactive for a very long time.
Contamination means radioactive material gets onto or inside the body; irradiation means the body is exposed to a radiation source nearby — these are different hazards.
Uses of radiation match the type of radiation to the job: gamma for medical imaging and sterilisation; alpha for smoke detectors; beta for thickness gauges.
intro: Every radioactive substance decays at its own characteristic rate. Some elements are so wildly unstable that they decay in milliseconds; others have been decaying since the Big Bang and will continue for billions of years. Half-life is how we measure this rate — and it turns out to be one of the most useful concepts in all of science. It lets archaeologists date bones that are tens of thousands of years old, doctors deliver targeted cancer treatments, and engineers sterilise surgical equipment. Understanding half-life also tells you how long radioactive waste stays dangerous — which is central to debates about nuclear power.

keyPoints.heading: What half-life means
keyPoints.content: Half-life is the time taken for the activity of a radioactive sample to halve. Equivalently, it is the time for half the undecayed nuclei in a sample to decay. Radioactive decay is random — you cannot predict which individual nucleus will decay next or when. But with very large numbers of atoms, the overall rate is statistically predictable. This is why half-life is a reliable measure even though individual decays are random. Activity (measured in becquerels, Bq) is the number of decays per second. After 1 half-life: activity = A₀/2. After 2 half-lives: activity = A₀/4. After 3 half-lives: activity = A₀/8. After n half-lives: activity = A₀ × (½)ⁿ. The number of undecayed nuclei halves at the same rate as the activity.
writeBullets: Half-life — time for activity (or number of undecayed nuclei) to halve
Activity measured in becquerels (Bq) = number of decays per second
Radioactive decay is random — you cannot predict individual decays, but the overall rate is predictable
After n half-lives: activity = initial activity × (½)ⁿ
Decay graph: curved line falling steeply then flattening — never reaches zero

keyTerms.def: The time for the activity of a radioactive sample (or number of undecayed nuclei) to halve
The number of radioactive decays per second; measured in becquerels (Bq)
The unit of radioactive activity; 1 Bq = 1 decay per second

keyPoints.memTip: Each half-life: divide activity by 2. After 3 half-lives: divide by 8 (= 2 × 2 × 2). After n half-lives: multiply by (½)ⁿ.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Give Mabel practice reading half-life from a graph — find a point, find half that value, read the time difference from the x-axis.

keyPoints.heading: Reading and drawing decay graphs
keyPoints.content: A radioactive decay graph shows activity (Bq) on the y-axis and time on the x-axis. The shape is always a curved decrease that approaches but never reaches zero — this is an exponential decay curve. To find the half-life from a graph: (1) pick any starting activity value (e.g. 800 Bq); (2) find half that value (400 Bq); (3) read the time at 800 Bq (t₁) and the time at 400 Bq (t₂); (4) half-life = t₂ − t₁. You can check by repeating from 400 Bq → 200 Bq: the time interval should be the same. To draw a decay graph: plot points at each half-life (0, t½, 2t½, 3t½, ...) with activity halving each time, then join with a smooth curve. The curve must never touch or cross the x-axis.
writeBullets: Decay graph: exponential curve — steep at first, flattening gradually, never reaching zero
To find half-life: pick an activity value, find half of it, read the time difference
Half-life is constant — same time interval to go from 800→400 Bq as from 400→200 Bq
To draw: halve activity at each half-life interval; join with smooth curve, never touching x-axis
Activity in Bq on y-axis; time (s, min, hours, or years) on x-axis

keyTerms.def: A curve where the quantity halves in equal time intervals — the shape of all radioactive decay graphs
A graph of activity vs time showing an exponential curve — used to determine half-life

keyPoints.memTip: Pick any two points where activity halves — the time between them is the half-life. Check it is the same anywhere on the curve.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Mabel should practise both reading half-life from graphs AND drawing decay curves — both come up in exams.

keyPoints.heading: Uses of radioactive isotopes
keyPoints.content: Different isotopes are chosen for different jobs based on their type of radiation and half-life. Carbon-14 dating: half-life ~5,700 years; beta emitter. Living things absorb carbon-14 (from CO₂); when they die, C-14 decays and is not replaced. Measuring remaining C-14 activity gives the age of organic remains — useful up to ~50,000 years. Medical tracers: radioactive isotope injected or swallowed, travels to the target organ, detected by gamma camera outside the body. Must be gamma emitter (penetrates body), short half-life (days — reduces radiation dose). Example: technetium-99m (half-life ~6 hours). Radiotherapy (cancer treatment): focused gamma beam kills cancer cells. Sterilisation: gamma radiation kills bacteria on surgical instruments — no chemicals needed. Smoke detectors: americium-241 (alpha emitter, half-life ~430 years) ionises air in the detector — when smoke blocks the ions, alarm triggers.
writeBullets: Carbon-14 dating — beta emitter, half-life 5,700 years; dates organic material up to ~50,000 years old
Medical tracers — gamma emitter, short half-life (days); injected and detected outside body
Technetium-99m — gamma, half-life ~6 hours; widely used medical tracer
Sterilisation — gamma radiation kills bacteria on surgical equipment without chemicals
Smoke detectors — americium-241, alpha emitter, half-life 430 years; ionises air; smoke breaks current → alarm

keyTerms.def: A radioactive isotope (gamma emitter, short half-life) used to track movement through the body and detected externally
Using the known half-life of C-14 (5,700 years) to determine the age of organic remains
A widely used medical gamma-emitting tracer with a half-life of ~6 hours

keyPoints.memTip: Tracer: gamma + short half-life. Smoke detector: alpha (Am-241). Carbon dating: beta, 5,700-year half-life.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Exam often asks 'why must the tracer have a short half-life?' — answer: to minimise the patient's total radiation dose.

keyPoints.heading: Contamination, irradiation and radiation dangers
keyPoints.content: Irradiation: being exposed to a source of radiation from outside the body. The source is not touching or inside you. Remove the source → irradiation stops. Example: standing near a radioactive rock. Contamination: radioactive material gets onto or into the body (skin, clothes, ingested, inhaled). The material keeps irradiating you even when moved away from other sources. Much harder to manage — particularly dangerous if it is an alpha emitter (highly ionising if inside body). Why radiation is dangerous: ionising radiation knocks electrons off atoms in body cells → damages DNA → can cause cancer (uncontrolled cell division) or genetic mutations (if reproductive cells are affected). High doses can cause radiation sickness (rapid cell death). Risk increases with dose and with the type of radiation (alpha most ionising — most dangerous inside body). Protection: distance, shielding (lead/concrete), minimise exposure time.
writeBullets: Irradiation — exposed to radiation source from outside; stops when source removed
Contamination — radioactive material on/in the body; continues to irradiate even when moved away
Ionising radiation damages DNA → cancer or genetic mutation
Alpha most dangerous if contamination — highly ionising near body cells
Protection: increase distance, use lead/concrete shielding, minimise time of exposure

keyTerms.def: Exposure to radiation from an external source — stops when the source is removed
Radioactive material that enters or contacts the body — continues to cause exposure even when removed from the radiation area

keyPoints.memTip: Irradiation = from outside, stops with distance. Contamination = on/in the body, stays with you. Contamination is harder to control.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Contamination vs irradiation is a classic exam distinction — Mabel should give an example of each and explain why contamination is more serious.

tables.title: Uses of radioactive isotopes
rows: Long enough to date ancient remains; can be measured precisely
Gamma penetrates body to be detected; short half-life reduces dose
Gamma penetrates to reach deep tumours; kills cancer cells
Gamma kills bacteria without making equipment radioactive
Alpha ionises air in detector; long half-life keeps detector working

0.question: A radioactive sample has an initial activity of 640 Bq. After 3 half-lives, what is the activity?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! After 3 half-lives: 640 → 320 → 160 → 80 Bq. Each half-life divides by 2, so after 3: 640 ÷ 8 = 80 Bq.
0.wrongFeedback: Divide by 2 three times: 640 → 320 → 160 → 80 Bq. Or use: 640 × (½)³ = 640 × (1/8) = 80 Bq.

1.question: Why must the radioactive tracer used in medical imaging have a short half-life?
options: To minimise the total radiation dose received by the patient
To make it easier to detect outside the body
To ensure the tracer reaches all organs equally
To prevent the tracer from decaying before it is injected
1.correctFeedback: Correct! A short half-life means the tracer quickly decays to a very low activity — the patient receives a much smaller total radiation dose.
1.wrongFeedback: The half-life must be short to minimise the total radiation dose — once the imaging is complete, you want the activity to fall quickly so the patient is not exposed to ongoing radiation.

3.question: What is the key difference between contamination and irradiation?
options: Contamination means radioactive material is on or in the body and continues to irradiate even when moved away from other sources; irradiation is exposure from an external source that stops when the source is removed
Contamination is always from gamma radiation; irradiation is always from alpha
Contamination only affects the skin; irradiation affects internal organs
Irradiation means the body becomes radioactive; contamination does not
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right! Contamination: radioactive material is on/in the body — the risk continues wherever you go. Irradiation: external source — remove the source or move away and the exposure stops.
3.wrongFeedback: The key distinction is location: contamination = radioactive material on/in the body (continues with you); irradiation = external source (stops when source removed or you move away).

commonMistakes: Dividing by n instead of (½)ⁿ — after 3 half-lives, divide by 8 (= 2³), not by 3.
Saying activity reaches zero — it never does; the curve approaches zero asymptotically but never arrives.
Confusing contamination and irradiation — contamination means radioactive material is on or in the body; irradiation is external exposure only.
Choosing an isotope with the wrong half-life for a medical tracer — a very long half-life gives an unnecessarily high radiation dose; too short and it decays before imaging is complete.
Forgetting to explain why the medical tracer must emit gamma — it needs to penetrate the body to be detected by a camera outside. Alpha or beta would be absorbed before reaching the detector.
examTips: To find half-life from a graph: pick a clear starting point, halve the activity, read both times from the x-axis, and subtract. Check your answer by repeating from the second point.
After n half-lives: remaining activity = initial × (½)ⁿ. Build a step-by-step table if you are not confident with powers.
For 'why gamma for a medical tracer' questions: (1) gamma penetrates tissues — detected outside body; (2) short half-life — dose falls quickly after the scan.
Contamination vs irradiation: contamination is harder to manage because the source stays with you — mention this when explaining why alpha contamination is so dangerous.
revisionCardBullets: Half-life: time for activity (or undecayed nuclei) to halve
Activity in becquerels (Bq) = decays per second
Decay graph: exponential curve — never touches zero
Carbon-14 dating: beta, half-life 5,700 years; organic remains up to ~50,000 years
Medical tracers: gamma emitter, short half-life (e.g. technetium-99m, 6 hours)
Sterilisation: gamma kills bacteria on surgical equipment
Smoke detector: americium-241, alpha emitter, half-life 430 years
Irradiation: external source — stops when source removed
Contamination: radioactive material on/in body — continues with you
Ionising radiation damages DNA → cancer or mutation
Protection: distance, lead/concrete shielding, minimise exposure time
